entries.title: What it is and why it matters
entries.body:
Encoding & Transformations are techniques that change the representation of data without changing its essence.
Typical examples: URL encoding, Base64, HTML entities, Unicode transformations.
They are used as gadgets to bypass input filters, evade a WAF, and break sanitization routines.
The idea: a payload in an "evasive" form passes the filter, and at the next processing stage is converted into a dangerous form.

entries.title: What Unicode and normalization are
entries.body:
Unicode is a universal standard for encoding characters from nearly all writing systems. Each character is assigned a unique code point (for example, U+0041 for "A"). The UTF-8 and UTF-16 formats define how the code points are stored in bytes.

Unicode normalization is the process of bringing text to a standardized, consistent form so that equivalent characters are represented identically in memory.

NFC  - Canonical Composition: assembles decomposed sequences into precomposed characters.
NFD  - Canonical Decomposition: splits characters into decomposed forms (base + combining marks).
NFKC - Compatibility Composition: like NFC, but additionally replaces characters with compatible equivalents (may change appearance/format).
NFKD - Compatibility Decomposition: like NFD, but additionally decomposes compatibility characters.

The attack idea: feed a "safe" Unicode character that, after normalization (often NFKC/NFKD), turns into dangerous ASCII and pierces the filter.

Mapping table: https://appcheck-ng.com/wp-content/uploads/unicode_normalization.html

entries.title: Checking normalization in all forms (Python)
entries.body:
import unicodedata
string = "ᴾᵃʸˡᵒᵃᵈˢ𝓐𝓵𝓵𝕋𝕙𝕖𝒯𝒽𝒾𝓃ℊ𝓈"
print ('NFC: ' + unicodedata.normalize('NFC', string))
print ('NFD: ' + unicodedata.normalize('NFD', string))
print ('NFKC: ' + unicodedata.normalize('NFKC', string))
print ('NFKD: ' + unicodedata.normalize('NFKD', string))

entries.title: What Punycode is
entries.body:
Punycode is a way to represent Unicode characters (non-ASCII letters, symbols, scripts) using only a limited set of ASCII (letters, digits, hyphens).

It is used mainly in DNS, which traditionally supports only ASCII. Punycode enables internationalized domain names (IDN): a domain can contain characters from many languages, translated into a safe ASCII form.

It is used for homograph attacks: a domain is visually indistinguishable from the real one but actually points to a different one.

entries.title: IDN domain in the browser -> actual ASCII (Punycode)
MySQL treats similar characters as equal
entries.body:
-- In MySQL similar characters are treated as equal.
-- Can be abused in Password Reset, Forgot Password and OAuth providers.
SELECT 'a' = 'ᵃ';
+-------------+
| 'a' = 'ᵃ'   |
+-------------+
|           1 |
+-------------+

entries.title: Disabling the trick via COLLATE utf8mb4_0900_as_cs
entries.body:
-- The trick works if the query uses COLLATE utf8mb4_0900_as_cs.
-- With this collation the characters are NO longer equal (result 0):
SELECT 'a' = 'ᵃ' COLLATE utf8mb4_0900_as_cs;
+----------------------------------------+
| 'a' = 'ᵃ' COLLATE utf8mb4_0900_as_cs   |
+----------------------------------------+
|                                      0 |
+----------------------------------------+

entries.title: What Base64 is
entries.body:
Base64 is a method of converting binary data (images, files) or text with special characters into a readable string of ASCII characters (A-Z, a-z, 0-9, + and /).
Every 3 input bytes are split into 4 groups of 6 bits and mapped to 4 Base64 characters.
If the input is not a multiple of 3 bytes, the output is padded with "=" characters (padding).

entries.title: Encoding and decoding (CLI)
entries.body:
echo -n admin | base64
YWRtaW4=

echo -n YWRtaW4= | base64 -d
admin
